draft.introduction: The Aitareya Upanishad opens with a creation account: the Ātman alone existed, then projected the worlds and created the cosmic person. Consciousness is identified as the foundation of all reality.

translation.text: In the beginning this was the Self alone, in the shape of a person. He looked around and saw nothing but himself. He first said, “I am.” From that the name I arose.
translation.original_text: ātmā vā idam eka evāgra āsīt | nānyat kiñcana miṣat

translation.text: He thought: “Let me now create the worlds.” He created these worlds: the water, the rays of light, death, and the waters.
translation.original_text: sa īkṣata lokān nu sṛjā iti | sa imāṃ lokān asṛjata

translator_notes.note: The phrase “ātmā vā idam eka” (“the Self alone was this”) establishes non-dual creation: the world emerges from the Self’s own being, not from external material. This is idealism in its purest form, centuries before Berkeley.
